meta.description: Temporal extensions of Condition Graphs encoding expected biomarker trajectories across disease phases. These templates serve as (1) question-generation infrastructure for CRES reasoning tasks, (2) answer-scoring rubrics for temporal grounding and trend consistency evaluation, and (3) a basis for constructing contrastive inference tasks using atypical presentations. They are EVALUATION INFRASTRUCTURE — not training data for models.
design_principles: Each phase has explicit time boundaries relative to a clinical anchor event
Biomarker trajectories specify direction, expected range, and rate of change
Atypical variants are encoded to support contrastive reasoning tasks
All values reference clinical guidelines or established literature
MIMIC-IV operationalisation is specified for each trajectory

trajectory_fields.marker: The biomarker or clinical variable being tracked
trajectory_fields.direction: Expected direction of change: rising | falling | stable | oscillating | absent→present | present→absent
trajectory_fields.expected_range: Typical value range during this phase (units specified)
trajectory_fields.rate: Expected rate of change: rapid | gradual | variable | plateau
trajectory_fields.mimic_source: How to operationalise this in MIMIC-IV

aki.name: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)
aki.anchor_event: First KDIGO criterion met (SCr ≥1.5× baseline or ↑≥0.3 mg/dL within 48h, or UO <0.5 mL/kg/h ×6h)
references: Kellum JA et al. Kidney Int 2012 (AKI trajectories)
KDIGO AKI/AKD Scope of Work 2023 (transient vs persistent vs progressive)
Matarneh et al. Renal Failure 2025 (biomarker timing)

phases.name: Pre-injury / Risk Window
phases.description: Subclinical injury period. Novel biomarkers may already be elevated while SCr remains within normal range. Haemodynamic insult or nephrotoxin exposure has occurred but functional markers have not yet responded.

trajectories.rate: slow (SCr lags actual GFR decline by 24-48h)
trajectories.clinical_significance: SCr insensitive in this phase; false reassurance risk

trajectories.expected_range: may exceed threshold (>150 ng/mL urine) 2-6h post insult
trajectories.clinical_significance: Earliest structural injury marker; precedes SCr rise by 24-48h
Cell cycle arrest marker; FDA-approved for AKI risk within 12h
UO often the first bedside signal; more sensitive than SCr for early AKI

trajectories.direction: may be low if pre-renal aetiology
trajectories.clinical_significance: Haemodynamic trigger for pre-renal AKI

trajectories.direction: may be rising if septic aetiology
trajectories.clinical_significance: Tissue hypoperfusion indicator; co-occurs with septic AKI risk

phases.name: Initiation / Onset Phase
phases.description: KDIGO criteria first met. SCr crosses diagnostic threshold. Injury mechanisms actively ongoing (ischaemia, nephrotoxin, inflammation). Metabolic consequences beginning to emerge.

trajectories.rate: rapid rise (0.3-0.5 mg/dL per 24h in severe cases)
trajectories.clinical_significance: Diagnostic threshold crossed; rate of rise predicts severity
Note: eGFR overestimates actual GFR during non-steady-state SCr rise

trajectories.expected_range: BUN:Cr ratio >20:1 suggests pre-renal; ≤15:1 suggests intrinsic
trajectories.clinical_significance: BUN:Cr ratio aids in distinguishing pre-renal from intrinsic AKI

trajectories.rate: may drop abruptly or gradually over 6-12h
trajectories.clinical_significance: Oliguric vs non-oliguric AKI: ~50% of AKI is non-oliguric (preserved UO)
Cardiac arrhythmia risk at >6.0; ECG monitoring indicated
Metabolic acidosis developing; anion gap may rise
Reduced renal excretion

trajectories.expected_range: may already be declining from 6-12h peak
trajectories.clinical_significance: Peak NGAL predicts severity; declining NGAL is favourable

phases.name: Extension / Oliguric-Anuric Phase
phases.time_relative_to_anchor: +24h to +72h (may extend to 7-14 days in severe ATN)
phases.description: Sustained kidney injury. Peak SCr reached or still rising. Oliguria or anuria in severe cases. Metabolic complications intensify. RRT may be initiated. Fluid overload risk is highest.

trajectories.clinical_significance: Peak SCr correlates with severity; time to peak predicts recovery trajectory
Anuria >12h = KDIGO Stage 3; RRT indication if life-threatening complications
Life-threatening: ECG changes (peaked T, wide QRS); emergency intervention needed

trajectories.expected_range: <18 mEq/L; may reach <12 in severe acidosis
trajectories.clinical_significance: Severe metabolic acidosis; Kussmaul respirations may develop

trajectories.rate: depends on fluid input vs output
trajectories.clinical_significance: Fluid overload → pulmonary oedema → hypoxia; a key driver of morbidity
Venous congestion worsens renal recovery; guides diuretic vs RRT decision
Due to ↓ 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production
Uremic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis) if BUN >100

phases.name: Maintenance / Plateau Phase
phases.time_relative_to_anchor: +3 to +14 days (variable; shortest in pre-renal, longest in ATN)
phases.description: SCr stabilises at elevated level. Tubular repair processes begin. GFR remains low but injury mechanisms are subsiding. Focus shifts to supportive care, avoiding second hits, and monitoring for recovery signals.

trajectories.clinical_significance: Stable SCr = injury mechanism ceased but repair not yet complete

trajectories.direction: earliest recovery signal: UO begins to increase
trajectories.clinical_significance: UO recovery often precedes SCr decline by 24-48h
Persistently elevated KIM-1 predicts incomplete recovery and CKD risk

phases.name: Recovery / Diuretic Phase
phases.time_relative_to_anchor: +7 to +21 days (onset highly variable; may not occur in non-recovery)
phases.description: Tubular function recovering. Urine output increases, sometimes dramatically (diuretic phase with polyuria). SCr begins declining toward baseline. Electrolyte instability from polyuria. Risk of volume depletion if output not matched.

trajectories.expected_range: declining toward baseline; may not return to pre-AKI level
trajectories.clinical_significance: Rate of SCr decline predicts completeness of recovery; note eGFR underestimates actual GFR during SCr fall
Polyuria reflects recovering tubules with impaired concentrating ability; hypovolaemia risk if not matched

trajectories.expected_range: may drop to <3.5 mEq/L during polyuria
trajectories.clinical_significance: Hypokalaemia risk during recovery; replace electrolytes

trajectories.expected_range: risk of hypernatraemia during polyuria if free water not replaced
trajectories.clinical_significance: Tubular concentrating defect → dilute urine
Acid-base normalisation

trajectories.expected_range: net negative 1-3 L/day during polyuric phase
trajectories.clinical_significance: Natural mobilisation of accumulated fluid; support with IV fluids if haemodynamically unstable

atypical_variants.name: Non-oliguric AKI
atypical_variants.deviation: Urine output remains >0.5 mL/kg/h throughout despite rising SCr and falling GFR
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: UO-based KDIGO criteria not met; SCr-only diagnosis. May be missed if only monitoring UO.
atypical_variants.cres_task: Contrastive: model must recognise AKI despite preserved urine output

atypical_variants.name: Rapidly reversible (pre-renal) AKI
atypical_variants.deviation: SCr peaks within 24-48h then declines rapidly with volume resuscitation. No sustained oliguric phase.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Transient AKI; full recovery expected. Persistent AKI (>48h) suggests transition to ATN.
atypical_variants.cres_task: Temporal grounding: model must distinguish transient from persistent trajectory

atypical_variants.name: AKI on CKD (acute-on-chronic)
atypical_variants.prevalence: ~30% of AKI in elderly ICU patients
atypical_variants.deviation: Elevated baseline SCr (e.g., 2.0 mg/dL). Absolute SCr rise smaller for same proportional GFR loss. Recovery may plateau above pre-AKI baseline, reflecting new CKD progression.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: KDIGO criteria still apply to proportional change; but absolute thresholds (e.g., >4.0 mg/dL) reached earlier
atypical_variants.cres_task: Threshold detection: model must correctly apply KDIGO staging relative to elevated baseline

atypical_variants.name: Delayed SCr rise (low muscle mass / liver disease)
atypical_variants.deviation: SCr rise blunted due to reduced creatinine generation (sarcopenia, cirrhosis). AKI severity underestimated.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Cystatin C or novel biomarkers more reliable in this population
atypical_variants.cres_task: Contrastive: model must recognise that 'normal' SCr can mask severe AKI

atypical_variants.name: Rhabdomyolysis-associated AKI
atypical_variants.deviation: SCr rises disproportionately fast (CK release adds to creatinine pool). Very high K⁺ and phosphate early. Myoglobin casts in urine.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: SCr rise partly artifactual (muscle creatine release); but kidney injury is real (cast nephropathy)
atypical_variants.cres_task: Diagnostic consistency: model must integrate CK, SCr, and UO to correctly attribute AKI cause

atypical_variants.name: Non-recovery / AKD transition
atypical_variants.deviation: SCr does not decline by day 7; meets AKD criteria (persistent abnormality 7-90 days). May require long-term RRT.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Transition from AKI to AKD; higher CKD conversion risk
atypical_variants.cres_task: Temporal reasoning: model must identify non-recovery trajectory and flag AKD transition

delirium.name: Delirium (ICU)
delirium.anchor_event: First CAM-ICU positive assessment (Feature 1 + 2 + either 3 or 4)
delirium.anchor_mimic: chartevents: first CAM-ICU positive result (RASS ≥ −3 required for assessment)
delirium.total_typical_duration: Median 2-3 days in ICU; may persist >7 days in 20-30% of cases
references: Pandharipande PP et al. Lancet 2013 (duration and cognitive outcomes)
Girard TD et al. NEJM 2018 (MIND-USA: haloperidol/ziprasidone)
Pisani MA et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2009 (delirium duration trajectories)

phases.name: Prodromal / Subclinical Phase
phases.description: Subsyndromal delirium. Patient may exhibit subtle signs: restlessness, mild inattention, sleep-wake cycle disruption. CAM-ICU may still be negative but ICDSC score trending upward. Metabolic or pharmacological precipitants accumulating.

trajectories.clinical_significance: Prodromal RASS instability predicts delirium onset within 24h
Sympathetic activation; autonomic instability precedes delirium
Circadian disruption is both risk factor and early sign

trajectories.direction: may be rising if infectious trigger
trajectories.clinical_significance: Neuroinflammatory pathway activation
Metabolic precipitants; correctable if identified early

phases.name: Acute Onset Phase
phases.description: Delirium diagnosed. Core features present: acute inattention, fluctuating consciousness, ±disorganised thinking or altered LOC. Motor subtype typically becomes apparent. Vital signs may show autonomic instability.

trajectories.clinical_significance: Definitive diagnosis. Begin PINCHME workup for reversible causes.

trajectories.expected_range: may swing between −3 and +3 within hours
trajectories.clinical_significance: Fluctuation = cardinal feature; distinguishes delirium from dementia/depression

trajectories.expected_range: 90-130 bpm (hyperactive); may be normal in hypoactive
trajectories.clinical_significance: Autonomic instability correlates with severity

trajectories.expected_range: labile; hypertension in hyperactive, hypotension in hypoactive
trajectories.clinical_significance: Autonomic dysregulation
Fever workup essential (infection is top precipitant)
Hypoxia both cause and consequence of delirium

phases.name: Fluctuating Course (Days 1-3)
phases.description: Characteristic waxing and waning of symptoms. Lucid intervals alternate with confusion. Motor subtype may shift (mixed most common). Circadian disruption prominent: sundowning pattern. Metabolic abnormalities may persist or resolve depending on treatment.

trajectories.expected_range: may be positive at night, negative in morning (sundowning)
trajectories.clinical_significance: THE hallmark temporal pattern of delirium; single time-point assessment may miss it

trajectories.expected_range: amplitude of fluctuation correlates with severity (CAM-ICU-7 score)
trajectories.clinical_significance: Greater RASS variability = more severe delirium trajectory
Attention is worst feature; letter recognition test errors increase
Circadian disruption perpetuates delirium; melatonin may help (PADIS 2025)

trajectories.direction: should be correcting if treatment effective
trajectories.clinical_significance: Failure to correct metabolic precipitants predicts prolonged delirium

phases.name: Resolution Phase
phases.time_relative_to_anchor: +48h to +7 days (variable; may be >7 days in persistent delirium)
phases.description: Gradual clearing of confusion. CAM-ICU becomes consistently negative. RASS stabilises at 0. Attention improves. Sleep-wake cycle normalises. Some patients retain subsyndromal symptoms for days to weeks.

trajectories.clinical_significance: Sustained CAM-ICU negativity × 24h = resolution
Stable RASS = resolution of motor disturbance
Autonomic stability restored

trajectories.direction: improving but may not return to pre-ICU baseline
trajectories.clinical_significance: Up to 40% of ICU delirium patients have persistent cognitive impairment at discharge

atypical_variants.name: Purely hypoactive delirium (missed delirium)
atypical_variants.prevalence: ~43% of ICU delirium; most commonly missed subtype
atypical_variants.deviation: No agitation, no psychomotor excitement. Patient appears withdrawn, drowsy, apathetic. RASS −1 to −3 but NOT sedation-related.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Often mistaken for depression, fatigue, or oversedation. Carries WORSE prognosis than hyperactive.
atypical_variants.cres_task: Contrastive: model must distinguish hypoactive delirium from appropriate sedation or depression

atypical_variants.name: Persistent delirium (>7 days)
atypical_variants.deviation: CAM-ICU remains positive beyond 7 days. No resolution phase. May evolve into chronic cognitive impairment.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Associated with dementia predisposition, higher mortality, longer ICU stay
atypical_variants.cres_task: Temporal reasoning: model must identify non-resolving trajectory and predict persistence

atypical_variants.name: Delirium superimposed on dementia
atypical_variants.prevalence: Common in elderly ICU patients (>65 years)
atypical_variants.deviation: Baseline cognitive impairment present. Fluctuation less pronounced. CAM-ICU Feature 1 (inattention) may be chronically present. New-onset disorganised thinking is the key distinguishing feature.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Higher delirium risk; worse outcomes; harder to detect
atypical_variants.cres_task: Diagnostic consistency: model must separate chronic dementia features from acute delirium features

atypical_variants.name: Alcohol withdrawal delirium (delirium tremens)
atypical_variants.deviation: Hyperadrenergic state: tremor, diaphoresis, seizures. Very high HR and BP. Distinct from typical ICU delirium trajectory — follows withdrawal timeline (24-72h after last drink).
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Requires benzodiazepines (unlike standard delirium where benzos are harmful)
atypical_variants.cres_task: Contrastive: model must recognise that benzodiazepines are THERAPEUTIC here but CAUSATIVE in typical delirium

atypical_variants.name: Rapid-cycling mixed subtype
atypical_variants.deviation: RASS alternates between positive and negative within hours. Shifts from agitation to withdrawal unpredictably.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Hardest to manage; medication titration challenging
atypical_variants.cres_task: Temporal grounding: model must recognise rapid RASS oscillation as mixed delirium, not artefact

stroke.name: Stroke (Ischaemic)
stroke.anchor_event: Symptom onset (last known well time) or, if wake-up stroke, time of symptom discovery
stroke.anchor_mimic: Earliest documented NIHSS assessment or nursing note indicating stroke symptoms
stroke.total_typical_duration: Acute phase: 0-72h; subacute: 72h-14 days; early chronic: 14 days+
references: AHA/ASA 2026 Guideline for Early Management of AIS (Prabhakaran et al.)
Tissue plasminogen activator for acute ischemic stroke (NINDS rt-PA Stroke Study Group, NEJM 1995)

phases.name: Hyperacute Phase (Ischaemic Penumbra)
phases.description: Core infarct surrounded by ischaemic penumbra (salvageable tissue). Neurological deficits maximal or progressing. Treatment decisions are time-critical: thrombolysis eligibility, EVT triage. Every minute of delay = further penumbral loss.

trajectories.expected_range: variable by territory; MCA LVO typically 10-25
trajectories.rate: may worsen in first hours (progressing stroke)
trajectories.clinical_significance: NIHSS drives treatment decisions: any disabling deficit → tPA eligible (AHA/ASA 2026)
Must lower to <185/110 before tPA; permissive hypertension if no tPA
AF detection critical for aetiology and secondary prevention
Hyperglycaemia worsens penumbral injury; must rule out hypoglycaemia (stroke mimic)

trajectories.expected_range: INR ≤1.7 and platelets ≥100K required for tPA
trajectories.clinical_significance: Coagulopathy = tPA contraindication

trajectories.direction: may be reduced in large territory stroke
trajectories.clinical_significance: Low GCS with LVO → urgent EVT consideration
Hypoxia exacerbates penumbral injury

phases.name: Early Acute Phase (Post-treatment / First 24h)
phases.description: Post-thrombolysis or post-EVT monitoring period. Neurological status may improve (reperfusion benefit) or worsen (haemorrhagic transformation). Strict BP control. Serial NIHSS monitoring. EVT still possible up to 24h for selected patients (DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria).

trajectories.direction: improving if successful reperfusion; stable or worsening if not
trajectories.rate: rapid improvement in first 2h post-tPA (dramatic responders); gradual over 24h
trajectories.clinical_significance: Early neurological improvement is strongest predictor of good outcome
BP excursions above target increase haemorrhagic transformation risk

trajectories.expected_range: any elevation suggests concurrent cardiac injury
trajectories.clinical_significance: Cardiac source workup; concurrent MI or takotsubo
Hyperthermia worsens infarct expansion; active cooling if >38°C

trajectories.direction: should be managed but NOT tightly
trajectories.expected_range: 140-180 mg/dL (AHA/ASA 2026: Class III harm for 80-130 target)
trajectories.rate: stable with insulin drip if needed
trajectories.clinical_significance: Both hypo- and hyperglycaemia worsen outcomes; moderate control preferred

trajectories.expected_range: assess for haemorrhagic transformation, oedema evolution
trajectories.clinical_significance: Symptomatic ICH occurs in ~6% post-tPA; routine CT at 24h

phases.name: Oedema / Complication Phase
phases.description: Cerebral oedema peaks. Risk of midline shift, herniation in large territory infarcts (malignant MCA syndrome). Neurological deterioration may occur despite initial improvement. Dysphagia screening, DVT prophylaxis, early mobilisation begin.

trajectories.clinical_significance: Secondary deterioration = urgent re-imaging; consider decompressive craniectomy if large MCA infarct and age <60

trajectories.expected_range: drop of ≥2 points from post-treatment baseline
trajectories.clinical_significance: GCS decline + pupil asymmetry = herniation → neurosurgical emergency
Cushing triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular breathing) = imminent herniation
Post-stroke fever within 72h associated with worse outcomes; treat aggressively
Post-stroke inflammatory response; also check for aspiration pneumonia
Hyponatraemia common post-stroke; worsens oedema

trajectories.expected_range: dysphagia present in 37-78% of acute stroke
trajectories.clinical_significance: Failed swallow screen → NPO, NG feeding; aspiration pneumonia prevention

phases.name: Stabilisation / Early Rehabilitation Phase
phases.description: Oedema resolving. Neurological status stabilising or gradually improving. Secondary prevention initiated (antiplatelet/anticoagulant, statin, antihypertensive). Early rehabilitation and mobilisation. Risk of recurrent stroke highest in first 2 weeks.

trajectories.clinical_significance: Rate of NIHSS improvement predicts functional outcome (mRS at 90 days)

trajectories.expected_range: target <140/90 (or <130/80 if tolerated) for secondary prevention
trajectories.clinical_significance: Long-term BP control reduces recurrence risk by ~30%
High-intensity statin = Class 1 recommendation (AHA/ASA 2026)
Diabetes management part of secondary prevention

trajectories.expected_range: ≥24h telemetry minimum; consider extended monitoring if cryptogenic
trajectories.clinical_significance: AF detection rate increases with monitoring duration; anticoagulation if AF found
Persistently elevated D-dimer → investigate for DVT (immobility risk)

atypical_variants.name: Wake-up stroke / Unknown onset time
atypical_variants.deviation: Onset time unknown (patient found with symptoms). Cannot apply standard time windows. DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI used to estimate onset window.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: May still be eligible for tPA if DWI-FLAIR mismatch present (WAKE-UP trial); EVT if perfusion mismatch
atypical_variants.cres_task: Temporal grounding: model must recognise that treatment eligibility requires imaging-based time estimation, not clock time alone

atypical_variants.name: Minor stroke with early neurological deterioration (END)
atypical_variants.deviation: Initial NIHSS low (2-4), then sudden worsening at 12-48h. May reflect clot propagation, re-occlusion, or oedema.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) within 24h reduces END risk (CHANCE/POINT trials)
atypical_variants.cres_task: Trend reasoning: model must flag unexpected NIHSS worsening from a low baseline

atypical_variants.name: Haemorrhagic transformation post-thrombolysis
atypical_variants.deviation: After initial improvement, sudden NIHSS worsening with headache, vomiting, BP spike. CT shows new haemorrhage.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Stop tPA infusion; reverse with cryoprecipitate/TXA; neurosurgical consult
atypical_variants.cres_task: Contrastive: model must distinguish oedema-related worsening from haemorrhagic worsening using vital sign and lab patterns

atypical_variants.name: Posterior circulation stroke (basilar artery)
atypical_variants.deviation: Atypical symptom profile: vertigo, diplopia, ataxia, bilateral weakness, bulbar symptoms. NIHSS underestimates severity (NIHSS skewed toward anterior circulation). GCS may drop rapidly.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: NIHSS ≥10 with basilar occlusion → EVT within 24h (AHA/ASA 2026 Class 1)
atypical_variants.cres_task: Diagnostic consistency: model must recognise NIHSS underestimation in posterior circulation and use additional signals

atypical_variants.name: Stroke mimic (hypoglycaemia, seizure, migraine)
atypical_variants.deviation: Focal neurological deficit resolves with glucose correction (hypoglycaemia), time (migraine aura), or is post-ictal. No infarct on imaging.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Glucose must be checked before tPA (AHA/ASA 2026: mandatory). tPA in stroke mimics has low bleeding risk but should be avoided.
atypical_variants.cres_task: Threshold detection: model must identify glucose <60 mg/dL as alternative explanation before attributing deficit to stroke

atypical_variants.name: Malignant MCA infarction
atypical_variants.deviation: Massive oedema with midline shift >5mm by 24-48h. NIHSS >20 with rapid deterioration despite treatment. GCS drops below 8.
atypical_variants.clinical_implication: Decompressive craniectomy within 48h reduces mortality from ~80% to ~30% in patients <60 years
atypical_variants.cres_task: Temporal reasoning: model must predict trajectory toward malignant oedema from early NIHSS, imaging, and clinical signs
